title: dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
text:
library(dplyr)

Warning: package 'dplyr' was built under R version 4.4.3

library(palmerpenguins)
This cheatsheet walks through converting old dplyr patterns to the new dplyr 1.2.0 functions. Each section shows the old way and the new way side-by-side.

title: dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
text:
Why Better?

✅ Clear intent (drop vs keep)
✅ Better NA handling (keeps NAs by default)
✅ No complex negation logic needed

title: dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
text:
Old Way

# Multiple | operators
penguins |>
  filter(
    (species == "Adelie" & island == "Torgersen" & body_mass_g > 3700) |
    (species == "Gentoo" & body_mass_g > 5000)
  ) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g)

# A tibble: 83 × 3
   species island    body_mass_g
   <fct>   <fct>           <int>
 1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
 2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
 3 Adelie  Torgersen        4675
 4 Adelie  Torgersen        4250
 5 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
 6 Adelie  Torgersen        4400
 7 Adelie  Torgersen        4500
 8 Adelie  Torgersen        4200
 9 Adelie  Torgersen        4450
10 Adelie  Torgersen        3900
# ℹ 73 more rows

title: dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
text:
New Way

# Clean OR logic with when_any()
penguins |>
  filter(
    when_any(
      when_all(species == "Adelie", island == "Torgersen", body_mass_g > 3700),
      when_all(species == "Gentoo", body_mass_g > 5000)
    )
  ) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g)

# A tibble: 83 × 3
   species island    body_mass_g
   <fct>   <fct>           <int>
 1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
 2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
 3 Adelie  Torgersen        4675
 4 Adelie  Torgersen        4250
 5 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
 6 Adelie  Torgersen        4400
 7 Adelie  Torgersen        4500
 8 Adelie  Torgersen        4200
 9 Adelie  Torgersen        4450
10 Adelie  Torgersen        3900
# ℹ 73 more rows

title: dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
text:
Why Better?

✅ Explicit OR logic (no need to mentally parse |)
✅ Nests cleanly with when_all()
✅ Easier to add/remove conditions

title: dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
text:
Old Way

# Simple OR with |
penguins |>
  filter(species == "Adelie" | body_mass_g > 5000) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g)

# A tibble: 213 × 3
   species island    body_mass_g
   <fct>   <fct>           <int>
 1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
 2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
 3 Adelie  Torgersen        3250
 4 Adelie  Torgersen          NA
 5 Adelie  Torgersen        3450
 6 Adelie  Torgersen        3650
 7 Adelie  Torgersen        3625
 8 Adelie  Torgersen        4675
 9 Adelie  Torgersen        3475
10 Adelie  Torgersen        4250
# ℹ 203 more rows

title: dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
text:
New Way

# Explicit OR
penguins |>
  filter(when_any(species == "Adelie", body_mass_g > 5000)) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g)

# A tibble: 213 × 3
   species island    body_mass_g
   <fct>   <fct>           <int>
 1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
 2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
 3 Adelie  Torgersen        3250
 4 Adelie  Torgersen          NA
 5 Adelie  Torgersen        3450
 6 Adelie  Torgersen        3650
 7 Adelie  Torgersen        3625
 8 Adelie  Torgersen        4675
 9 Adelie  Torgersen        3475
10 Adelie  Torgersen        4250
# ℹ 203 more rows

title: dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
text:
Why Better?

✅ Clearer intent (especially for newcomers)
✅ Consistent with nested when_any() patterns

title: dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
text:
Old Way

# Repetitive == checks
penguins |>
  slice(1:4) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g) |>
  mutate(
    island_location = case_when(
      island == "Biscoe" ~ "Southwest",
      island == "Dream" ~ "Northwest",
      island == "Torgersen" ~ "Northwest",
      .default = island
    )
  )

# A tibble: 4 × 4
  species island    body_mass_g island_location
  <fct>   <fct>           <int> <chr>          
1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750 Northwest      
2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800 Northwest      
3 Adelie  Torgersen        3250 Northwest      
4 Adelie  Torgersen          NA Northwest

title: dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
text:
Why Better?

✅ No repetitive == checks
✅ Clearer intent (recoding values)
✅ Can use lookup tables
✅ Better error handling with unmatched = "error"

title: dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
text:
Old Way

# Manual case_when from vectors
island_mapping <- list(
  "Biscoe" = "Southwest",
  "Dream" = "Northwest",
  "Torgersen" = "Northwest"
)

# Have to construct case_when manually
penguins |>
  slice(1:4) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g) |>
  mutate(
    island_location = case_when(
      island == "Biscoe" ~ island_mapping[["Biscoe"]],
      island == "Dream" ~ island_mapping[["Dream"]],
      island == "Torgersen" ~ island_mapping[["Torgersen"]],
      .default = island
    )
  )

# A tibble: 4 × 4
  species island    body_mass_g island_location
  <fct>   <fct>           <int> <chr>          
1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750 Northwest      
2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800 Northwest      
3 Adelie  Torgersen        3250 Northwest      
4 Adelie  Torgersen          NA Northwest

title: dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
text:
Why Better?

✅ Lookup tables stored as data frames
✅ Easy to maintain and update mappings
✅ Can load from CSV or database

title: dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
text:
Why Better?

✅ No .default boilerplate
✅ Clear intent (replacing specific values)
✅ Unmatched values automatically preserved

title: dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
text:
Why Better?

✅ No .default needed
✅ Clear that only specific values are being replaced
✅ Works with any number of replacements

title: dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
text:
Old Way

# Manual validation needed
islands <- c("Biscoe", "Dream", "UnknownIsland")
data <- tibble(island = islands)

# case_when() silently returns NA for unmatched
result <- data |>
  mutate(
    location = case_when(
      island == "Biscoe" ~ "Southwest",
      island == "Dream" ~ "Northwest"
      # UnknownIsland becomes NA - no error!
    )
  )

print(result)

# A tibble: 3 × 2
  island        location 
  <chr>         <chr>    
1 Biscoe        Southwest
2 Dream         Northwest
3 UnknownIsland <NA>     

# Would need to manually check for NAs
if (any(is.na(result$location))) {
  stop("Unmatched values found!")
}

Error: Unmatched values found!

title: dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
text:
New Way

# Automatic validation
islands <- c("Biscoe", "Dream", "UnknownIsland")
data <- tibble(island = islands)

# Errors immediately on unmatched value
data |>
  mutate(
    location = island |>
      recode_values(
        "Biscoe" ~ "Southwest",
        "Dream" ~ "Northwest",
        unmatched = "error"  # Explicit error handling
      )
  )

Error in `mutate()`:
ℹ In argument: `location = recode_values(...)`.
Caused by error in `recode_values()`:
! Each location must be matched.
✖ Location 3 is unmatched.

title: dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet
text:
Why Better?

✅ Fail fast on data issues
✅ Explicit error handling
✅ No silent NA creation

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
dplyr functions
So many helpful functions!


⬢ distinct()
⬢ slice()
⬢ count()
⬢ pull()
⬢ relocate()
⬢ rename()
⬢ *_join()
⬢ …

But for now, let’s focus on:
⬢ filter()
⬢ mutate() + case_when()


dplyr.tidyverse.org

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
Quick review of filter()
filter() picks cases based on their values







 

Image generated by tidy extensions

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
Quick review of filter()
filter() picks cases based on their values







   

Image generated by tidy extensions

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
filter() is ambiguous!
monthly_losses_data |>
  filter(island == "Torgersen")

Are you keeping (filtering in) Torgersen or dropping (filtering out) Torgersen?

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
section: filter() is optimized for keeping rows, but dropping rows can require complex logic
text:
filter() is optimized for keeping rows, but dropping rows can require complex logic
Example: Drop rows where island is Torgersen and body mass is greater than 4000g.







    

Using filter() with negation drops BOTH the target row AND rows with NA!

Image generated by tidy extensions

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
section: The problem with using filter() to exclude
text:
The problem with using filter() to exclude
To properly use filter(), we would need to do something like:

penguins_NA |>
  filter(
    !((island == "Torgersen" & !is.na(island)) &
             (body_mass_g > 4000 & !is.na(body_mass_g)))
    )

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
Work smarter with filter_out()
filter_out() drops rows where ALL conditions match (keeps NAs!)







   

Image generated by tidy extensions

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
section: When to use filter() vs filter_out()
text:
When to use filter() vs filter_out()


Use filter() to keep rows
penguins |>
  filter(species == "Adelie")
✅ Positive logic
✅ What you want to keep

Use filter_out() to drop rows
penguins |>
  filter_out(species == "Adelie")
✅ Negative logic
✅ What you want to exclude


Rule of thumb: If you’re using ! in your filter(), consider filter_out() instead!

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
Confusing logic statements
Keep rows where Adelie penguins from Torgersen have body mass over 3700g OR where Gentoo penguins have body mass over 5000g.







   

Image generated by tidy extensions

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
section: Work smarter with filter() + when_any() example
text:
Work smarter with filter() + when_any() example
when_any() cleanly expresses OR logic - keeps rows matching at least one condition




  

Image generated by tidy extensions

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
Work smarter with filter() + when_any()
More powerful when you have complex combinations to keep







  

Image generated by tidy extensions

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
Work smarter with filter() + when_all()
when_all() cleanly expresses AND logic - keeps only rows matching ALL conditions







  

Image generated by tidy extensions

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
Layering when_any() + when_all()
The real power: nest when_all() inside when_any() for complex logic!







  

Image generated by tidy extensions

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
section: When to use when_any() vs when_all()
text:
When to use when_any() vs when_all()


Use when_any() for OR logic
penguins |>
  filter(when_any(
    species == "Adelie",
    body_mass_g > 5000
  ))
✅ At least one condition must be true
✅ Cleaner than nested | operators

Use when_all() for AND logic
penguins |>
  filter(when_all(
    species == "Gentoo",
    body_mass_g > 5000
  ))
✅ All conditions must be true
✅ Useful when nesting inside when_any()
✅ For simple AND, just use commas!


Rule of thumb: If you have complex | or & expressions in your filter(), consider when_any() and when_all()!

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
Quick review of mutate()
mutate() adds new columns or modifies existing ones

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
Quick review of mutate() + case_when()
case_when() evaluates conditions in order and creates a new column

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
case_when() and unmatched rows
Unmatched rows default to NA

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
section: case_when() wasn’t meant to recode values
text:
case_when() wasn’t meant to recode values
case_when() evaluates multiple conditions to recode island names to locations

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
section: Use recode_values() when recoding and matching with values
text:
Use recode_values() when recoding and matching with values
recode_values() matches exact values and recodes them to new values

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
section: Use recode_values() when recoding and matching with values
text:
Use recode_values() when recoding and matching with values
With unmatched = "error", operation stops on first unmatched value

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
section: Using recode_values() and a lookup table
text:
Using recode_values() and a lookup table
The lookup table provides the mapping from old values to new values

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
When to use recode_values()


Use case_when() for conditions
penguins |>
  mutate(size = case_when(
    body_mass_g > 4500 ~ "Large",
    body_mass_g < 3500 ~ "Small",
    .default = "Medium"
  ))
✅ Complex logical conditions
✅ Comparing values with >, <, >=, etc.

Use recode_values() for exact matches
penguins |>
  mutate(location = island |>
    recode_values(
      "Biscoe" ~ "Southwest",
      "Dream" ~ "Northwest",
      "Torgersen" ~ "Northwest"
    ))
✅ Simple == equality checks
✅ Many values to recode
✅ Can use a lookup table!


Rule of thumb: If you’re using == in case_when(), consider recode_values() instead!

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
New replace_values() function
replace_values() replaces specific values (no need for .default)

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
New replace_when() function
replace_when() replaces values based on a condition (no .default needed!)

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
When to use replace_*()


Use case_when()/recode_values() to create a new column
penguins |>
  mutate(size = case_when(
    body_mass_g > 4500 ~ "Large",
    body_mass_g < 3500 ~ "Small",
    .default = "Medium"
  ))
✅ Building a new variable from scratch
✅ Multiple outcomes based on conditions
✅ Requires .default for unmatched cases

Use replace_*() to modify an existing column

penguins |>
  mutate(body_mass_g =
    replace_when(body_mass_g,
      body_mass_g > 5000 ~ 5000
    ))
✅ Replacing specific values in a column
✅ Unmatched values stay unchanged
✅ No .default needed!


Rule of thumb: If you want to modify some values but keep the rest, use replace_*() instead of case_when()/recode_values() with .default!

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text: case_match() has been soft deprecated

Use an MCP server

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
Community feedback is sooo important



We are looking for #rstats community feedback on 3 new dplyr functions!
We're aiming to expand the filter() family:

filter() to keep rows
filter_out() to drop rows
when_any() and when_all() as modifiers

Read more and leave feedback here: github.com/tidyverse/ti…[image or embed]

— Davis Vaughan (@davisvaughan.bsky.social) November 7, 2025 at 10:03 AM

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text: Tidyups repo

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text: Community feedback is sooo important

title: Working smarter with dplyr 1.2.0
text:
Rules of thumb
⬢ Use filter() to keep rows
⬢ Use filter_out() to drop rows (if you’re using ! in filter())
⬢ Use when_any() for OR logic (if you have complex | expressions)
⬢ Use when_all() for AND logic (useful when nesting inside when_any())
⬢ Use recode_values() for exact matches (if you’re using == in case_when())
⬢ Use replace_*() to modify some existing columns

install.packages("pak")
pak::pak("dplyr")

title: Old Patterns - Pre-dplyr 1.2.0
text:
library(dplyr)

Warning: package 'dplyr' was built under R version 4.4.3

library(palmerpenguins)
This document shows working examples of the “old” ways of doing common data manipulation tasks that dplyr 1.2.0’s new functions are designed to improve.

title: Old Patterns - Pre-dplyr 1.2.0
text:
Filtering Out Rows (Old Way)
Problem: Using ! negation with filter() to drop rows

# Drop rows where species is Adelie
penguins |>
  filter(!(species == "Adelie")) |>
  count(species)

# A tibble: 2 × 2
  species       n
  <fct>     <int>
1 Chinstrap    68
2 Gentoo      124


Problem with NA handling:

# Create data with NA
penguins_na <- penguins |>
  mutate(island = if_else(row_number() == 1, NA_character_, island))

# Using ! drops both the target AND NAs!
penguins_na |>
  filter(!(island == "Torgersen" & body_mass_g > 4000))

# A tibble: 332 × 8
   species island    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g
   <fct>   <chr>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>       <int>
 1 Adelie  <NA>                39.1          18.7               181        3750
 2 Adelie  Torgersen           39.5          17.4               186        3800
 3 Adelie  Torgersen           40.3          18                 195        3250
 4 Adelie  Torgersen           36.7          19.3               193        3450
 5 Adelie  Torgersen           39.3          20.6               190        3650
 6 Adelie  Torgersen           38.9          17.8               181        3625
 7 Adelie  Torgersen           34.1          18.1               193        3475
 8 Adelie  Torgersen           37.8          17.1               186        3300
 9 Adelie  Torgersen           37.8          17.3               180        3700
10 Adelie  Torgersen           41.1          17.6               182        3200
# ℹ 322 more rows
# ℹ 2 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>


Correct but complex workaround:

penguins_na |>
  filter(
    !((island == "Torgersen" & !is.na(island)) &
      (body_mass_g > 4000 & !is.na(body_mass_g)))
  )

# A tibble: 333 × 8
   species island    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g
   <fct>   <chr>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>       <int>
 1 Adelie  <NA>                39.1          18.7               181        3750
 2 Adelie  Torgersen           39.5          17.4               186        3800
 3 Adelie  Torgersen           40.3          18                 195        3250
 4 Adelie  Torgersen           NA            NA                  NA          NA
 5 Adelie  Torgersen           36.7          19.3               193        3450
 6 Adelie  Torgersen           39.3          20.6               190        3650
 7 Adelie  Torgersen           38.9          17.8               181        3625
 8 Adelie  Torgersen           34.1          18.1               193        3475
 9 Adelie  Torgersen           37.8          17.1               186        3300
10 Adelie  Torgersen           37.8          17.3               180        3700
# ℹ 323 more rows
# ℹ 2 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>

title: Old Patterns - Pre-dplyr 1.2.0
text:
Complex OR Logic (Old Way)
Problem: Multiple | operators become hard to read

# Keep Adelie from Torgersen with body mass > 3700g OR Gentoo with body mass > 5000g
penguins |>
  filter(
    (species == "Adelie" & island == "Torgersen" & body_mass_g > 3700) |
    (species == "Gentoo" & body_mass_g > 5000)
  ) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g)

# A tibble: 83 × 3
   species island    body_mass_g
   <fct>   <fct>           <int>
 1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
 2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
 3 Adelie  Torgersen        4675
 4 Adelie  Torgersen        4250
 5 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
 6 Adelie  Torgersen        4400
 7 Adelie  Torgersen        4500
 8 Adelie  Torgersen        4200
 9 Adelie  Torgersen        4450
10 Adelie  Torgersen        3900
# ℹ 73 more rows

title: Old Patterns - Pre-dplyr 1.2.0
text:
Complex AND Logic (Old Way)
Problem: Nested conditions are hard to track

# Keep only Adelie from Torgersen with body mass > 3700g
penguins |>
  filter(
    species == "Adelie" &
    island == "Torgersen" &
    body_mass_g > 3700
  ) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g)

# A tibble: 22 × 3
   species island    body_mass_g
   <fct>   <fct>           <int>
 1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
 2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
 3 Adelie  Torgersen        4675
 4 Adelie  Torgersen        4250
 5 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
 6 Adelie  Torgersen        4400
 7 Adelie  Torgersen        4500
 8 Adelie  Torgersen        4200
 9 Adelie  Torgersen        4450
10 Adelie  Torgersen        3900
# ℹ 12 more rows


Nested OR and AND:

# Complex: (Adelie from Torgersen with mass > 3700) OR (Gentoo from Biscoe)
penguins |>
  filter(
    ((species == "Adelie" & island == "Torgersen") & body_mass_g > 3700) |
    (species == "Gentoo" & island == "Biscoe")
  ) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g)

# A tibble: 146 × 3
   species island    body_mass_g
   <fct>   <fct>           <int>
 1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
 2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
 3 Adelie  Torgersen        4675
 4 Adelie  Torgersen        4250
 5 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
 6 Adelie  Torgersen        4400
 7 Adelie  Torgersen        4500
 8 Adelie  Torgersen        4200
 9 Adelie  Torgersen        4450
10 Adelie  Torgersen        3900
# ℹ 136 more rows

title: Old Patterns - Pre-dplyr 1.2.0
Old Patterns - Pre-dplyr 1.2.0
section: Using case_match() (Old Way - Now Soft Deprecated)
text:
Using case_match() (Old Way - Now Soft Deprecated)
Problem: case_match() is being deprecated in favor of recode_values()

penguins |>
  slice(1:4) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g) |>
  mutate(
    island_location = case_match(
      island,
      "Biscoe" ~ "Southwest",
      "Dream" ~ "Northwest",
      "Torgersen" ~ "Northwest",
      .default = island
    )
  )

Warning: There was 1 warning in `mutate()`.
ℹ In argument: `island_location = case_match(...)`.
Caused by warning:
! `case_match()` was deprecated in dplyr 1.2.0.
ℹ Please use `recode_values()` instead.


# A tibble: 4 × 4
  species island    body_mass_g island_location
  <fct>   <fct>           <int> <chr>          
1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750 Northwest      
2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800 Northwest      
3 Adelie  Torgersen        3250 Northwest      
4 Adelie  Torgersen          NA Northwest

title: Old Patterns - Pre-dplyr 1.2.0
Old Patterns - Pre-dplyr 1.2.0
text:
Summary of Pain Points

filter() with !: Complex NA handling, unclear intent
Complex | and &: Hard to read nested logic
case_when() for exact matches: Repetitive == checks
case_when() for replacements: Unnecessary .default boilerplate
case_match(): Being deprecated, used for simple value mapping

All of these patterns work, but dplyr 1.2.0 provides clearer, more expressive alternatives!
